intro_text:
Anse Lazio is a 600-metre crescent of white sand on the northwest tip of Praslin, the second-largest island of the Seychelles, closed at each end by rounded granite boulders and backed by takamaka trees. It is one of the most-voted beaches on the planet: TripAdvisor's Travellers' Choice put it third in the world in 2017 and sixth in 2024, CNN ranked it fourth in 2016, and Lonely Planet calls it the best beach on Praslin. The boulders are the signature of the inner Seychelles — weathered grey-and-pink granite, the eroded remains of the Gondwana supercontinent, not the volcanic or coral rock of most tropical islands.

The single fact that sets Anse Lazio apart from almost every other Seychelles beach is what it lacks: a fringing coral reef. Most beaches in the archipelago sit behind a reef that breaks the swell and pens a shallow turquoise lagoon. Anse Lazio has none. The bay opens straight onto the Indian Ocean, so the water is clearer and deepens quickly a short way out, and the swell and current arrive with nothing to slow them down. The clarity that draws swimmers and the depth that endangers them are the same condition.

There is no road onto the sand. You drive the Anse Lazio road to a car park near Chevalier Bay, then cross a steep hill on foot and walk down — short, but a real climb back out in the heat. Two restaurants sit on the sand itself: Le Chevalier Bay at the northern end and Bonbon Plume, a thatched open-air place open only for lunch that fills its tables fast. Beyond them there is no resort on the beach; the hotels are inland and around the coast at Anse Volbert and Cote d'Or.

The uncomfortable part of the record is specific and dated. In August 2011, two visitors were killed by sharks in the bay 16 days apart: a 36-year-old French diver, Nicolas Virolle, on 1 August, and British honeymooner Ian Redmond, 30, snorkelling about 20 metres offshore on 16 August as his wife watched from the beach. Swimming was banned at several Praslin bays, then 150-metre safety nets were installed; they were removed in 2016 because swimmers ignored them and the monsoon kept tearing them apart. There are no lifeguards.

Praslin's other draw is half an hour inland: the Vallee de Mai, a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1983 and one of only two places on Earth where the coco de mer palm — bearer of the largest seed in the plant kingdom — grows wild. The other is Curieuse Island, off Praslin's north coast. Anse Lazio is the beach you reach over a hill; the forest of the world's strangest palm is why the island was worth landing on at all.
spike_explainer:
The thing that makes Anse Lazio singular is an absence shared by almost nothing else in the Seychelles: there is no fringing coral reef in front of it.

To understand why that matters, you have to know how most Seychelles beaches work. The granitic inner islands are typically ringed by a coral reef that sits offshore and breaks the incoming Indian Ocean swell. Behind that reef the water is shallow, warm and still — a lagoon you can wade into for a hundred metres without the sea reaching your chest. It is calm, forgiving, and at low tide often too shallow to properly swim. Anse Lazio does not have this. The bay on Praslin's northwest tip opens directly onto open ocean with no reef barrier, so the seabed shelves away quickly and the water is genuinely deep a short distance from the sand.

**This single difference explains both why people love the beach and why it can kill them.** Without a reef stirring up sediment in a shallow lagoon, the water at Anse Lazio is clearer and a deeper blue than the milky turquoise of a reef-flat. You can actually swim here — properly swim, not shuffle through ankle-deep water — which is rarer in the Seychelles than visitors expect. But the same open exposure means the swell, the wind-driven chop and the currents reach the shore with nothing to break them. The local guidance is consistent and blunt: currents strengthen quickly, especially near the granite boulders at either end of the bay, and there are no lifeguards. The clear deep water that makes Anse Lazio the best swimming beach on Praslin is the clear deep water that has no reef holding the ocean back.

**The boulders that frame the beach are not decoration; they are the bones of the island.** The rounded grey-and-pink granite of Anse Lazio, and of the Seychelles generally, is continental rock — the eroded remnant of the Gondwana supercontinent, stranded in mid-ocean when India tore away from Africa and Madagascar tens of millions of years ago. This is why the inner Seychelles look unlike almost any other tropical archipelago. Most tropical islands are young: volcanic cones or low coral cays built up from the sea. The granitic Seychelles are old continental crust, weathered into boulders that pile at the ends of beaches like Anse Lazio. The takamaka trees (Calophyllum) leaning over the sand to throw shade complete the picture, but it is the granite that tells you where you are.

**The beach also resists the thing that usually ruins a famous coastline: a road and a strip of resorts.** There is no vehicle access onto the sand at Anse Lazio. You drive the Anse Lazio road to a car park near Chevalier Bay, then cross a steep hill on foot and walk down to the beach. It is a short crossing, but it is a genuine climb on the way back out in tropical heat, and it keeps the immediate hinterland of the beach undeveloped. The only commerce on the sand is two restaurants — Le Chevalier Bay at the northern end and the lunch-only, thatch-roofed Bonbon Plume — both serving Creole grilled fish, curries and seafood. There is no hotel on the beach. Accommodation is inland or around the coast at Anse Volbert and Cote d'Or, which is why Anse Lazio still reads as a natural bay rather than a resort frontage even on its busiest day.

**And the rankings are real, repeated, and worth stating precisely rather than waving at.** TripAdvisor's Travellers' Choice 'best of the best' beaches put Anse Lazio third in the world in 2017 and sixth in 2024; CNN ranked it fourth in its world top-ten in 2016; the World's 50 Best Beaches list placed it 29th in 2023; and Lonely Planet calls it the best beach on Praslin and among the best in the archipelago. These are aggregations of traveller reviews and editorial picks, not a single objective measurement, and the rank wobbles year to year — but the pattern across a decade and several different juries is consistent enough to take seriously.

That is the whole argument for Anse Lazio. It is one of the most consistently celebrated beaches in the world because it offers what most Seychelles beaches cannot — clear, deep, properly swimmable water inside a granite-framed bay reachable only on foot. The price of every one of those qualities is the missing reef: deep water with a current, two restaurants and no lifeguard, and a record, dated to August 2011, that the postcards leave out.
honest_reckoning_note: Anse Lazio's defining feature is the reason it has a death toll. With no fringing reef to break the swell, the water deepens fast and the current strengthens quickly, especially around the granite boulders at each end — and there are no lifeguards. In August 2011 two visitors were killed by sharks in the bay within 16 days: a 36-year-old French diver, Nicolas Virolle, on 1 August, and a British honeymooner, Ian Redmond, snorkelling about 20 metres offshore on 16 August while his wife watched from the sand. Swimming was banned at several Praslin bays, then 150-metre safety nets were strung across Anse Lazio; they were removed in 2016 after swimmers routinely ignored them and the northwest monsoon kept destroying them. The beach is now designated non-motorized. The quieter cost is access and pressure: the Seychelles is one of the most expensive island destinations on Earth, so the crowd is filtered by money before the hill filters it by effort, and a 600-metre beach with two restaurants and a single car park absorbs the popularity that a third-in-the-world ranking brings. None of this is on the postcard, and all of it is true.
reckoning_pullquote: The clear, deep, reef-free water that makes Anse Lazio the best swimming beach on Praslin is the same water that killed two people 16 days apart in August 2011 — and there are no lifeguards.

key_facts.label: Length and setting
key_facts.value: About 600 metres of white sand on the northwest tip of Praslin, closed at both ends by granite boulders and backed by takamaka trees
key_facts.source: Story Seychelles; Lonely Planet; Wikipedia: Anse Lazio

key_facts.label: No coral reef
key_facts.value: Unlike most Seychelles beaches, Anse Lazio has no fringing reef; the bay opens directly onto the Indian Ocean, so the water is clearer and deepens quickly

key_facts.label: Rankings
key_facts.value: TripAdvisor Travellers' Choice: 3rd in the world (2017), 6th (2024); CNN world top-ten: 4th (2016); World's 50 Best Beaches: 29th (2023); Lonely Planet: best on Praslin
key_facts.source: Seychelles News Agency; CNN; Worlds50Beaches; Lonely Planet

key_facts.label: 2011 shark fatalities
key_facts.value: Two visitors killed in the bay 16 days apart in August 2011: French diver Nicolas Virolle (1 Aug) and British honeymooner Ian Redmond, 30 (16 Aug)
key_facts.source: Seychelles News Agency; CBS News; Gulf News

key_facts.label: Safety nets
key_facts.value: 150-metre nets installed at Anse Lazio after the 2011 attacks were removed in 2016 — swimmers ignored them and the northwest monsoon kept tearing them apart
key_facts.source: Seychelles News Agency; Wikipedia: Anse Lazio

key_facts.label: No lifeguards, real currents
key_facts.value: There are no lifeguards; currents strengthen quickly, especially near the boulders at either end. The beach is now designated non-motorized

key_facts.label: Granite geology
key_facts.value: The boulders are weathered grey-pink continental granite — Gondwana remnant rock, not volcanic or coral — the geological signature of the inner Seychelles

key_facts.label: Vallee de Mai and the coco de mer
key_facts.value: Inland on Praslin: the Vallee de Mai, UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1983, one of only two places the coco de mer palm grows wild (the other is nearby Curieuse Island)
key_facts.source: UNESCO World Heritage Centre (#261); Lonely Planet

margin_notes.text: The boulders are not coral or lava. The inner Seychelles are continental granite — a fragment of the Gondwana supercontinent left in mid-ocean when India broke from Africa and Madagascar. That ancient rock, weathered round, is why these islands look like nowhere else in the tropics.

No reef means clearer, deeper water you can actually swim in — and a swell and current with nothing to break them. The clarity that draws you out is the depth that can hold you. Currents run hardest near the boulders at each end; there are no lifeguards.

Two restaurants sit on the sand: Le Chevalier Bay at the north end and Bonbon Plume, a thatched open-air place open only for lunch, with the best tables under umbrellas in the sand. Tables go fast — book Bonbon Plume ahead. There is no hotel on the beach.

August 2011 is the part the rankings omit: two people killed by sharks here 16 days apart. Seychelles banned swimming, then netted the bay; the nets came out in 2016 because people swam past them and the monsoon destroyed them.

Half an hour inland is the Vallee de Mai, a UNESCO site since 1983 and one of just two places the coco de mer — the largest seed of any plant — grows wild. The other is Curieuse Island, visible off Praslin's north coast.

day_in_time.dawn: At first light the car park near Chevalier Bay is empty and the hill crossing keeps it that way a while longer. The boulders at the north and south ends catch the early sun warm and pink before the day-trippers arrive, and the water — reef-free and clear — is at its calmest, though calm here still means open ocean with no lifeguard watching it.
day_in_time.midday: By late morning the beach has the people who made the walk over the hill, and Bonbon Plume is filling its lunch tables in the sand. The middle of the bay is where people swim; the official advice is to stay clear of the boulders at either end, where the current runs hardest. Takamaka shade at the back of the beach is the only relief from a sun the Seychelles does not soften.
day_in_time.golden: Late afternoon light rakes down the 600-metre crescent and across the granite, the photographers' hour and the reason Anse Lazio appears on so many lists. As Bonbon Plume closes after its lunch-only service and people start the climb back over the hill, the beach thins. The water often calms toward evening, but the depth and current do not go away with the crowd.
day_in_time.night: After dark Anse Lazio is unlit and effectively closed — no road onto the sand, no resort frontage, no lifeguard, the restaurants shut. The hotels are inland at Anse Volbert and Cote d'Or, where Praslin's quiet nightlife such as it is happens. This is not a beach to be on at night; the same emptiness that makes it photogenic by day makes it remote in the dark.

food_drink.name: Bonbon Plume
food_drink.description: A thatched, open-air restaurant of wood and palm-leaf standing directly on Anse Lazio's sand, with its best tables set under umbrellas on the beach. Creole cooking and grilled seafood — lobster, fish, scallops, mussels Seychellois with rice, salad and lentils. Open for lunch only, and tables go fast, so booking is advised.
food_drink.where: On the sand at Anse Lazio

food_drink.name: Le Chevalier Bay Restaurant
food_drink.description: A beachfront restaurant at the northern (Chevalier Bay) end of Anse Lazio serving Creole staples — grilled fish, garlic prawns, fish curry — across breakfast, lunch and dinner. One of only two food options actually on the beach; the rest of Praslin's eating is inland and around the coast.
food_drink.where: Northern end of Anse Lazio, near the car park

food_drink.name: Creole seafood and coco de mer country
food_drink.description: Praslin's cooking is Seychellois Creole: grilled and curried reef fish, octopus curry, breadfruit, and seafood landed locally. Prices reflect that the Seychelles imports most of what it does not catch and is one of the world's most expensive island destinations — a simple lunch on the sand is not a budget meal.
food_drink.where: Anse Lazio and across Praslin (Anse Volbert, Cote d'Or)

timeline.title: Lazare Picault names Praslin 'Ile des Palmes'
timeline.description: The French explorer Lazare Picault, charting the Indian Ocean for Mahe de La Bourdonnais, named Praslin 'Ile des Palmes' for its palm forests — without realising he had found the only wild home of the coco de mer. The island was renamed Praslin in 1768 after the duc de Praslin.

timeline.title: Vallee de Mai inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site
timeline.description: The Vallee de Mai, the palm forest at the heart of Praslin and one of only two places the coco de mer grows wild, was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1983 — at about 19.5 hectares, one of the smallest natural World Heritage Sites in the world.

timeline.title: First fatal shark attack: Nicolas Virolle
timeline.description: On 1 August 2011 a 36-year-old French diver, Nicolas Virolle, was killed by a shark while in the water at Anse Lazio — the first fatal shark attack recorded in the Seychelles since 1963.

timeline.title: Second fatal shark attack: Ian Redmond
timeline.description: On 16 August 2011, 16 days after the first death, British honeymooner Ian Redmond, 30, was killed by a shark while snorkelling about 20 metres off Anse Lazio as his wife watched from the beach. The Seychelles authorities banned swimming at several Praslin bays.

timeline.title: Swim ban lifted; safety nets installed
timeline.description: The swimming ban imposed after the August 2011 attacks was lifted in early 2012, and 150-metre safety nets were strung across the bay at Anse Lazio to enclose a protected swimming area.
timeline.source: Shark Attack Survivors; Seychelles News Agency

timeline.title: Safety nets removed
timeline.description: Five years after the attacks, the Seychelles Maritime Safety Authority removed the nets, saying they no longer served their purpose: swimmers routinely went outside them despite warnings, and the northwest monsoon's heavy waves kept damaging them.

timeline.title: CNN ranks Anse Lazio 4th in the world
timeline.description: CNN placed Anse Lazio fourth in its list of the world's top ten beaches in 2016, one of a run of high rankings across the decade that kept the beach in global travel coverage.

timeline.title: TripAdvisor Travellers' Choice ranks it 3rd in the world
timeline.description: TripAdvisor's Travellers' Choice 'best of the best' beach award ranked Anse Lazio third in the world in 2017, scoring it a perfect ten for untouched beauty in the underlying review analysis.

timeline.title: TripAdvisor ranks it 6th; beach designated non-motorized
timeline.description: TripAdvisor's 2024 Travellers' Choice award again placed Anse Lazio sixth in the world. By 2024 the beach was designated non-motorized, keeping jet-skis and powered watercraft off the bay.
timeline.source: Seychelles News Agency; Wikipedia: Anse Lazio

zones.name: Chevalier Bay end (northern boulders)
zones.character: The northern end of the crescent, near the Chevalier Bay car park and the arrival path over the hill. Granite boulders pile into the water here, with Le Chevalier Bay restaurant set back on the sand.
zones.best_for: Arriving, eating, shade, the first view down over the bay
zones.notes: Currents run hard around the boulders; this is not the safest stretch to swim. Stay to the open middle of the beach for swimming.

zones.name: Central crescent
zones.character: The wide central sweep of white sand, takamaka shade at the back and open clear water in front. With no reef, the water deepens quickly and is genuinely swimmable — the heart of the beach.
zones.best_for: Swimming, sunbathing, the postcard view of granite and turquoise
zones.notes: No lifeguards. Watch the conditions before you swim and keep clear of the boulders at either end where the current is strongest.

zones.name: Southern boulders toward Anse Georgette
zones.character: The southern granite headland closing the bay, beyond which a coastal path leads toward Anse Georgette, another celebrated Praslin beach to the north of the island's tip.
zones.best_for: Photographs of the boulders, walking on toward Anse Georgette
zones.notes: The boulders are slippery and the current here is strong; treat the rocky ends as scenery, not swimming water.

landmarks.name: The Anse Lazio granite boulders
landmarks.description: The weathered grey-and-pink granite boulders closing each end of the bay — the eroded continental rock of the inner Seychelles, a Gondwana remnant rather than volcanic or coral stone. They are the beach's visual signature and the reason the current runs hardest at the ends.

landmarks.name: Vallee de Mai
landmarks.description: The palm forest at the centre of Praslin, a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1983 and one of only two places the coco de mer palm grows wild. About 19.5 hectares, it is one of the smallest natural World Heritage Sites in the world, half an hour's drive inland from Anse Lazio.

landmarks.name: Curieuse Island
landmarks.description: The island off Praslin's north coast, visible from this side of Praslin, and the only place other than the Vallee de Mai where the coco de mer grows naturally. Now a marine national park, it is a common boat-trip pairing with a day at Anse Lazio.

cultural_refs.title: TripAdvisor Travellers' Choice 'Best of the Best' Beaches
cultural_refs.creator: TripAdvisor
cultural_refs.description: TripAdvisor's traveller-voted beach award ranked Anse Lazio third in the world in 2017 and sixth in 2024, the clearest evidence of its standing in popular travel culture. The rankings aggregate traveller reviews rather than measure anything objective, and the rank shifts year to year.

cultural_refs.title: CNN World's Top 10 Beaches (2016)
cultural_refs.creator: CNN
cultural_refs.description: CNN placed Anse Lazio fourth in its 2016 list of the world's ten best beaches, one of several editorial rankings that kept Praslin in international travel media through the 2010s.

cultural_refs.title: The World's 50 Best Beaches (2023)
cultural_refs.creator: World's 50 Best Beaches
cultural_refs.description: Anse Lazio was placed 29th in the 2023 World's 50 Best Beaches list, voted by a panel of travel experts and influencers — a more modest rank than its TripAdvisor and CNN placings, and a reminder that 'best beach' lists disagree.

cultural_refs.title: Lonely Planet Seychelles
cultural_refs.creator: Lonely Planet
cultural_refs.description: Lonely Planet calls Anse Lazio the best beach on Praslin and among the best in the whole archipelago — an editorial judgement repeated across editions of its Seychelles coverage.

recurring_events.name: Northwest monsoon swell season
recurring_events.description: During the northwest monsoon the open, reef-free bay takes heavier waves from the northwest — the same swell that repeatedly destroyed the safety nets installed after 2011. Conditions and the current change with the season, which is part of why there is no permanent net.

recurring_events.name: Peak visitor season
recurring_events.when_text: December to March and the April-May shoulder
recurring_events.description: Praslin's busiest months bring the most day-trippers over the hill to a single 600-metre beach with one car park and two restaurants. A third-in-the-world ranking concentrates demand onto a small space, and the car park and Bonbon Plume's lunch tables fill early.

businesses.name: Bonbon Plume
businesses.description: Thatched open-air Creole and seafood restaurant directly on Anse Lazio's sand, with tables under umbrellas on the beach. Open for lunch only; booking advised as tables fill fast. Listed by Seychelles tourism sources.

businesses.name: Le Chevalier Bay Restaurant
businesses.description: Beachfront restaurant at the northern (Chevalier Bay) end of Anse Lazio serving Creole cuisine — grilled fish, garlic prawns, fish curry — across breakfast, lunch and dinner. Listed by Seychelles tourism sources.
businesses.address: Anse Lazio (Chevalier Bay end), Praslin, Seychelles

things_to_know.label: No road onto the sand; a hill to cross
things_to_know.note: Drive the Anse Lazio road to the car park near Chevalier Bay, then cross a steep hill on foot and walk down to the beach. It is short but a real climb back out in the heat. There is no vehicle access onto the sand.

things_to_know.label: No reef, no lifeguard, real current
things_to_know.note: Anse Lazio has no fringing reef, so the water deepens quickly and the current strengthens fast, especially near the granite boulders at each end. There are no lifeguards. Swim in the open middle of the bay, check conditions first, and don't swim alone.

things_to_know.label: The 2011 record is real
things_to_know.note: Two people were killed by sharks in this bay in August 2011. Swimming was banned, then netted, and the nets were removed in 2016. Shark attacks here are extremely rare in the long record, but the bay is open ocean — take the water seriously.

things_to_know.label: The Seychelles is expensive
things_to_know.note: The Seychelles is one of the world's costliest island destinations. There is no hotel on Anse Lazio itself; the two beach restaurants are not budget meals, and reaching Praslin from Mahe means a ferry or a domestic flight on top of an international fare.

access_note: Anse Lazio is on the northwest tip of Praslin, about 3 km from Praslin Island Airport (PRI). There is no road onto the sand: drive the Anse Lazio road to the car park near Chevalier Bay, then cross a steep hill on foot and walk a short way down to the beach — easy going down, a real climb back up in the heat. Reach Praslin from Mahe (Seychelles International Airport, SEZ) either by the Cat Cocos ferry (about an hour, arriving at Baie Ste Anne, then a taxi across the island) or by a roughly 15-minute Air Seychelles domestic flight to PRI. Two restaurants sit on the beach — Le Chevalier Bay at the north end and the lunch-only Bonbon Plume — but there is no hotel on Anse Lazio; accommodation is inland and around the coast at Anse Volbert and Cote d'Or. There are no lifeguards.
